data.title: UBUYHolders issues 5.40B shares to AEI, AEI now owns 95% of common stock
data.description:
UBUYHolders issued 5,402,988,580 common shares to AEI after conversion of 10,000,000 Series A-1 preferred shares, resulting in AEI owning 95% of the company.

Key Highlights:

  • Issued 5,402,988,580 common shares on March 30, 2026, following a Notice of Conversion.
  • Conversion was of 10,000,000 Series A-1 preferred shares owned by AEI Capital Ltd.
  • AEI, the company's principal shareholder, now holds 95% of issued and outstanding common stock.
  • Company CEO John Tan Honjian is the principal shareholder of AEI, linking control to management.

Original SEC Filing: UBUYHOLDINGS INC [ UBYH ] - 8-K - Apr. 08, 2026

Disclaimer
This is an AI-powered summary. It may contain inaccuracies. Consider verifying important information with the source. Please note this summary is solely based on documents filed with the SEC.

data.title: These Companies Pay 5% Dividend Yields, and They're Hiking Them Too — Barrons.com
data.description:
By Ian Salisbury

With uncertainty still hanging over the markets, confidence is in short supply. Dividend hikes often signal company managers are bullish enough to sign up for a long-term commitment to return cash to shareholders.

One way for investors to own companies that consistently raise payouts is through an ETF, like the Vanguard Dividend Appreciation ETF. The $117 billion index fund targets companies that have raised dividends for at least 10 years.

There is a problem. Plenty of growth-oriented tech companies make minuscule payouts that they raise regularly but don't offer investors much in the way of income. Indeed, the fund's top holdings include Broadcom (with a yield of 0.8%); Apple (0.4%) and Eli Lilly (0.7%). Overall, the ETF boasts a yield of 1.6%, only slightly better than the S&P 500's anemic 1.2%.

To find something more appealing to income investors, we looked for stocks with higher yields where managers were still committed to increasing annual payouts. Our FactSet screen includes names with dividend yields of at least 5% that have increased their dividend payout rate at least once in the past five quarters. To screen out names with shaky financials, we also limited the list to companies whose dividends consume no more than 80% of profits and which Wall Street analysts expect to generate at least some profit growth this year.

In this low-yield market, the list generated just three names: Verizon Communications; Las Vegas focused real estate investment trust VICI Properties; and retailer Best Buy. All three stocks have had their issues lately — that's inevitable when you hunt for yields that are so large relative to the market. But they also offer potential value.

Shares of Verizon are up 16% so far this year, although the telecom has badly lagged the market over the past five years thanks to intense competition and pricing pressure. Last October lead director Dan Schulman took the reins from CEO Hans Vestberg. Less than three months later, the company announced its largest-ever round of layoffs, cutting more than 13,000 jobs. In January, Verizon made headlines again, when a service interruption hit more than 180,000 customers.

Still, investors may be betting the company has turned the corner. Shares jumped nearly 12% on Jan. 30, when it reported better-than-expected fourth-quarter subscriber growth and revenue. Analysts expect earnings to grow 4% in 2026. Despite the recent gains, Verizon trades at less than 10 times forward earnings.

Verizon's dividend yield stands at 5.8%, after the company hiked its quarterly payout to 71 cents a share from 69 cents in January. The dividend's full-year cost of $2.84 a share looks solid, on pace to eat up only about 58% of Verizon's estimated $4.89 in 2026 profits.

Best Buy and VICI Properties could also be appealing turnaround stories. Best Buy's struggles in the e-commerce world are well known. But Wall Street analysts expect the company to eke out profit growth of 1% in 2026. The company, which increased its dividend by a penny to 96 cents last month, yields 6%.

VICI Properties, which owns a number of Las Vegas's key attractions, such as Caesars Palace and the Venetian Resort, has seen its share price slump as that city's casinos struggle to compete with online gaming. But as Barron's latest cover story noted , it's way too early to write Sin City off. Wall Street analysts forecast VICI's adjusted funds from operations, a real estate equivalent to operating profit, to grow 10% in 2026. The stock yields 6.5%.

Write to Ian Salisbury at ian.salisbury@barrons.com

This content was created by Barron's, which is operated by Dow Jones & Co. Barron's is published independently from Dow Jones Newswires and The Wall Street Journal.

data.title: CBOT wheat hits 1-month low as crude dives and US weather improves
data.description:
Chicago Board of Trade wheat futures fell to a one-month low on Wednesday after U.S. President Donald Trump agreed to a two-week ceasefire with Iran, sending crude oil prices tumbling.

  • CBOT May soft red winter wheat (WK26) settled down 17-3/4 cents at $5.80-1/4 per bushel after dipping to $5.77, the contract's lowest since March 5.

  • K.C. May hard red winter wheat (KWK26) ended down 12-1/4 cents at $5.95-1/4 a bushel and Minneapolis May spring wheat (MWEK26) fell 17 cents to finish at $6.24 a bushel.

  • The break in crude oil appeared to trigger long liquidation in grain markets, analysts said. Managed commodity funds had established a small net long position in CBOT wheat futures by the week ended March 31, U.S. regulatory data showed, leaving the market open to long liquidation.

  • Wheat faced additional pressure from improving U.S. crop prospects, with rains expected in much of the central and southern Plains through this weekend.

  • Traders continued to digest a rising forecast of wheat output in Russia, the world's top exporter. Commodity data firm Argus on Tuesday raised its forecast of Russia's 2026/27 wheat crop to 88.7 million metric tons, from its November prediction of 86.5 million tons.

  • Ahead of the U.S. Department of Agriculture's monthly supply/demand report on Thursday, analysts surveyed by Reuters on average expected the agency to slightly trim its forecast of U.S. 2025/26 wheat ending stocks and modestly raise its forecast of world wheat inventories.

  • Ahead of Thursday's weekly USDA export sales report, traders expected the government to report U.S. wheat sales in the week to April 2 at zero to 150,000 tons of old-crop wheat and 150,000 to 400,000 tons of new-crop wheat.

data.title: Apple Turns To Samsung For Foldable Screens
data.description:
Apple is reportedly turning to Samsung Display as its sole supplier for foldable iPhone screens, a sign that the technology still isn't widely ready across the industry.

The report suggests Apple has agreed to a 3-year exclusive deal for foldable OLED panels, mainly because other suppliers just aren't there yet. BOE's foldable displays are seen as not meeting Apple's standards, while LG Display hasn't scaled production for foldable smartphone panels. That leaves Samsung as the only realistic option for now, even though Apple usually prefers working with multiple suppliers to keep costs down and maintain flexibility. This exclusivity could run until 2029 or 2030, depending on how quickly competitors catch up.

On the product side, Apple appears to be taking a measured approach. Shipments are expected to reach around 3 million units by the end of 2026, well below earlier projections of 8 million to 10 million units. Pricing is also expected to be premium, starting near $2,325, which suggests this will be more of a high-end, early-adopter device rather than a mass-market push.

data.title: FedEx Freight Spinoff Targets 12% Margin
data.description:
FedEx (FDX, Financials) is pressing forward with plans to spin off its freight division. The standalone trucking business is aiming for a 12% operating margin as it gets ready to become its own company.

FedEx Freight, which will split up on June 1, is expected to make around $8.7 billion in sales and $1.1 billion in adjusted operating profits this year, according to comments made by incoming CEO John Smith at the unit's maiden investor day.

The company, which specializes in less-than-truckload (LTL) shipping, also predicted that its operating profit would expand by 10% to 12% a year over the next few years, while its sales would climb by 4% to 6%. Companies like XPO, Saia, and Old Dominion Freight Line compete with this part of the business.

Analysts have been saying for a long time that FedEx Freight's worth wasn't fully reflected in the larger FedEx structure. It might have greater freedom to grow and make more money in a competitive trucking industry if it is a separate firm.

But the forecast is not as good because of headwinds in the business, such as increasing diesel costs that are still hurting profits across the board.

Investors will keep an eye on how well the new independent firm does after it opens and whether it can meet its growth and profitability goals.

data.title: Meta Stock Rises After Launch of New AI Model — WSJ
data.description:
By Jared Mitovich

Shares of Meta Platforms rose 6% after the company announced its first major new artificial-intelligence model in more than a year.

The new model, called Muse Spark, will power Meta's AI chatbot and AI features within it. It comes as Meta has spent billions of dollars hiring AI talent while it plays catch-up to OpenAI, Anthropic, and Google DeepMind.

Meta's stock pared back some of its gains in early morning trading in line with the major stock indexes. It rose as much as 9.5%, the stock's largest intraday percent increase since January, according to Dow Jones Market Data.

This item is part of a Wall Street Journal live coverage event. The full stream can be found by searching P/WSJL (WSJ Live Coverage).

data.title: Airlines Face Mixed Signals As Oil Drops
data.description:
Delta Air Lines and the broader airline space may be getting a boost from lower oil prices, but Bernstein says the story isn't that simple yet.

Speaking on CNBC, analyst David Vernon said cheaper fuel is a positive, but it won't immediately show up in airline earnings. There's a lag, as refined fuel still moves through global supply chains before costs fully normalize. In the meantime, what really matters is whether airlines can hold onto the higher fares and fees they've recently pushed through.

That's where things get tricky. Vernon pointed out that no airline is completely insulated from economic pressure, even Delta with its premium-heavy customer base. If business travel budgets start tightening, demand could soften just as costs are easing. Delta does have some buffers, though, including its Trainer refinery, which offsets roughly one-third of fuel cost swings, along with support from maintenance and cargo operations.

data.title: Oil prices notch biggest daily drop in almost 6 years as cease-fire deal offers two weeks of uncertainty
data.description:
By Myra P. Saefong and Isabel Wang

U.S. oil prices are still over 40% higher than before the Iran war, while Brent crude has risen 31% since late February

President Donald Trump said Washington and Tehran had agreed to a "double sided CEASEFIRE!"

Oil prices posted their biggest one-day percentage decline in almost six years on Wednesday after the U.S. and Iran agreed to a two-week cease-fire, but U.S. and global benchmark crude ended above their sessions lows as Iran maintained control of the Strait of Hormuz.

Iran, meanwhile, has reportedly accused Israel of breaking the cease-fire deal with its attacks on Lebanon. As a result, Iran's Fars News Agency said in a post on X that the passage of oil tankers through the Strait of Hormuz has been halted. White House press secretary Karoline Leavitt, meanwhile, said reports that Iran had shut down the strait were "false."

Read: Iran moves to put the brakes on reopening the Strait of Hormuz

"It's not surprising to see oil prices come down in the manner that they have," said Tariq Zahir, managing member at Tyche Capital Advisors. However, they are still substantially higher from when the war started, he noted.

This cease fire is 'two weeks of uncertainty, and we could be in the same exact place in two weeks.' Tariq Zahir, Tyche Capital Advisors

"Until we see real clarity on the strait reopening to everyone and the negotiations that start late this week, we do not feel we will see prices continue their downward track to prewar levels," he told MarketWatch.

"We need clarity on the strait, we need clarity on nuclear stockpiles and clarity on ballistic missiles. This cease-fire offers "two weeks of uncertainty, and we could be in the same exact place in two weeks," Zahir added.

U.S. benchmark West Texas Intermediate crude for May delivery (CLK26) (CL.1) fell 16.4%, to settle at $94.41 a barrel, after trading as low as $91.03 Wednesday. That marked its largest one-day percentage decline since April 27, 2020, according to Dow Jones Market Data.

June Brent crude (BRNM26) (BRN00) fell 13.3% to end at $94.75 a barrel, paring its decline for the session after touching an intraday low of $90.40. It logged its largest daily percentage fall since April 21, 2020.

The decline in oil prices comes after U.S. President Donald Trump on Tuesday evening announced in a post on Truth Social that Washington and Tehran had agreed to a cease-fire. The deal would allow for the Strait of Hormuz to reopen for two weeks, while the U.S. would suspend attacks on Iran.

The agreement was reached less than two hours before Trump's deadline of 8 p.m. Eastern time Tuesday, when he warned that Iran's "whole civilization will die" if the Islamic Republic did not accept his demands.

Still, it "seems like the U.S. told Iran that it would halt bombing, Iran said OK, and apart from that nothing else has been clarified," Gregory Brew, senior oil analyst at Eurasia Group wrote in a post on X. "Hormuz, GCC attacks, Israel-Lebanon. All still up in the air." GCC refers to the Gulf Cooperation Council, whose members include Saudi Arabia, the UAE, Kuwait, Qatar, Bahrain and Oman.

"[The] oil market will release that soon enough," Brew wrote.

Read: Here's when gas prices and airfares could go down if the Iran cease-fire holds

Key questions remain when it comes to oil, including how quickly transit through the Strait of Hormuz can resume, how long will it take to resupply importers, whether the cease-fire will hold, and whether Iran will "capitalize on the opportunity to assert strength over the strait," said Rob Haworth, senior investment strategy director at U.S. Bank Asset Management.

He pointed out that there's a "physical limitation to normalization" when it comes to Asian supply and demand, because the trip to Asia from the Middle East takes 30 days each way. Also, there's the question of whether infrastructure damage in the Gulf will limit export capabilities if ships arrive to fill and resupply global inventories, said Haworth.

Oil prices remained significantly higher than before the U.S. and Israel first struck Iran at the end of February. WTI crude is still almost 41% higher than it was at the start of the war, while Brent crude has risen nearly 31% since then, according to Dow Jones Market Data.

Bettors on prediction-market platform Polymarket have wagered a 27% chance that traffic through the route will return to normal by the end of April, down from 65% one month ago. (Polymarket has a data partnership with Dow Jones, the publisher of MarketWatch.)

See: The cease-fire mirage: Here's why stock-market bulls may already be getting ahead of themselves

U.S. benchmark stock indexes surged in Wednesday dealings. The Dow Jones Industrial Average DJIA was up 2.5%, while S&P 500 SPX was rising 2.2% and the Nasdaq Composite COMP added 2.5%.

Read: The real reason stocks are rallying so much after the Iran cease-fire

Nora Redmond contributed.

-Myra P. Saefong -Isabel Wang

This content was created by MarketWatch, which is operated by Dow Jones & Co. MarketWatch is published independently from Dow Jones Newswires and The Wall Street Journal.

data.title: IBM In Focus After Analyst Price Target Trim
data.description:
IBM is back in the spotlight ahead of its Q1 results, with Needham lowering its price target to $290 from $340 as it adjusts for a more mixed outlook.

Analyst David Grossman said the firm made a few tweaks to its 2026 forecasts, mainly to reflect some pressure on software and services tied to geopolitical tensions, along with currency headwinds. At the same time, there are some offsets, including the earlier-than-expected closing of the Confluent acquisition. Needham now sees constant currency revenue growth of 4.5% to 5.0%, a touch below guidance, while modeling EPS of $12.38, up 7%, and free cash flow growth of about $1 bilion, also up 7%.

As for the upcoming Q1 report on April 22, Grossman expects things to come in largely as expected. The quarter is typically a softer one for IBM, and given the early stage of the year and a still uncertain macro backdrop, he doesn't see much changing in terms of full-year guidance.

data.description:

Margin expansion is targeted via supply chain efficiencies, private brand growth, and a retail media network. Market share gains are supported by third-party data, and broad-based category strength is expected to drive transaction growth.

data.title: US Economic Fundamentals Remain ‘Solid’ Despite Middle East War, Fed’s Daly Reportedly Says
data.description:
Key points:
  • Daly reportedly said that consumers are still spending, businesses are still investing, and the labor market is settling. 
  • The Fed President also added that answers to questions like the war’s impact and its effect on oil as well as inflation would depend on how long the conflict lasted.
  • Meanwhile, minutes from the Fed’s March meeting revealed that many policymakers saw a case for possible interest rate hikes if inflation stayed above the 2% target.

San Francisco Federal Reserve President ‌Mary Daly reportedly said on Wednesday that the U.S. economy’s fundamentals were looking strong amid growing consumer spending, business investments, and stabilizing labor market despite the war in the Middle East.

According to a report from Reuters, Daly said at the St. ​George Area Chamber of Commerce in Utah that while the impact of the war is yet to be seen, the U.S. economy is “really in a good place” so far.

"What we've seen ​is consumers are still spending, businesses are still investing," Daly reportedly said.

"There's a concern that maybe this ⁠will push inflation up: that's our job, we'll focus on that. And there's a ​concern that maybe the labor market isn't as solid, but we're not seeing that, we're ​seeing it kind of settle at a good place," the Fed President added.

Iran War Impact

The war in Iran is at a critical phase after about 40 days of attacks, with a two-week ceasefire being negotiated.

"The question is what's going to happen with the war? How ​long will prices of oil and gas stay elevated and what ​will ...the knock-on ⁠effects be in terms of other goods and services?" Daly reportedly said to Reuters, adding that answers to these questions would depend on how long the conflict would last.

"Importantly, we do know ⁠that ​the underlying fundamentals of the economy remain solid and ​those are things that are important to how we see inflation progressing over time and how we see the ​labor market progressing," Daly said.

What About Interest Rates?

Meanwhile, minutes from the Federal Reserve’s March meeting released on Wednesday revealed that a growing number of policymakers saw a case for possible interest rate hikes if inflation remains above the 2% target, particularly amid inflationary pressures linked to the U.S.-Israel war with Iran.

While the Fed held rates at 3.50%–3.75% in its latest policy update, many participants continued to expect rate cuts, noting that a prolonged conflict in the Middle East could weaken growth, soften labor market conditions, and reduce purchasing power due to higher oil prices.

Meanwhile, at the time of writing, the SPDR S&P 500 ETF (SPY), which tracks the S&P 500 index, gained 2.28%, the Invesco QQQ Trust ETF (QQQ) surged 2.6%, and the SPDR Dow Jones Industrial Average ETF Trust (DIA) rose 2.52% amid anticipation for a ceasefire.

The United States Oil Fund ETF (USO) declined about 10%, while the ProShares Ultra Bloomberg Crude Oil ETF (UCO) was down about 2%.

data.title: Treasuries Give Back Ground After Early Surge But Remain Firmly Positive
data.description:
WASHINGTON (dpa-AFX) - After ending the previous session little changed, treasuries showed a strong move to the upside during trading on Wednesday.

Bond prices gave back ground after an early surge but remained firmly positive. Subsequently, the yield on the benchmark ten-year note, which moves opposite of its price, slid 5.2 basis points to 4.291 percent after hitting a low of 4.234 percent.

The early rally by treasuries came in reaction to news that the U.S., Israel and Iran have agreed to a two-week ceasefire.

In a Truth Social post Tuesday evening, President Donald Trump said he has agreed to suspend the bombing and attack of Iran for a period of two weeks subject to Tehran agreeing to the complete, immediate and safe opening of the Strait of Hormuz.

Trump said the U.S. has received a 10-point proposal from Iran that he believes is a 'workable basis on which to negotiate' and said the two-week ceasefire will allow the agreement to be finalized and consummated.

A subsequent statement from Iran's Foreign Minister Abbas Araghchi indicated the Strait of Hormuz will be reopened for a period of two weeks if the attacks against Iran are halted.

The news has contributed to a nosedive by the price of crude oil, with U.S. crude oil futures plunging by more than 15 percent and dropping well below $100 a barrel.

While the conflict in the Middle East has led to a heightened degree of economic uncertainty, the minutes of the Federal Reserve's latest monetary policy meeting revealed many officials still expect to resume cutting interest rates this year.

The minutes of the Fed's March 17-18 meeting said participants emphasized the importance of being 'nimble' in adjusting the stance of monetary policy

'Many participants judged that, in time, it would likely become appropriate to lower the target range for the federal funds rate if inflation were to decline in line with their expectations,' the Fed said.

However, the Fed noted a couple of these participants had pushed their assessment of the most likely timing of rate cuts further into the future in light of recent readings on inflation.

The comments in the minutes come as officials' latest economic projections provided after the meeting showed they still expect to cut interest rates by a quarter point this year.

Copyright(c) 2026 RTTNews.com. All Rights Reserved

Copyright RTT News/dpa-AFX

data.title: Wheat Futures Drop After Cease-Fire Reached — Daily Grain Highlights
data.description:
By Kirk Maltais

  • Wheat for July delivery fell 2.8% to $5.91 a bushel on the Chicago Board of Trade on Wednesday, with the risk premium in grains stepping back after a temporary cease-fire was reached in the conflict with Iran.
  • Corn for July delivery fell 0.5% to $4.57 3/4 a bushel.
  • Soybeans for July delivery rose 0.2% to $11.77 1/4 a bushel.

HIGHLIGHTS

Making Moves: Fund traders are continuing to liquidate long positions in grain futures, extending this week's trend, after President Trump said he agreed to a cease-fire with Iran if the Strait of Hormuz reopened immediately. The cease-fire pressured wheat futures as the market watched to see if Iran would allow more ships through the maritime chokepoint.

Damage Done: Investors welcomed the cease-fire with Iran, but significant issues with fertilizer availability are already hitting grains, said Jake Hanley of Teucrium Trading. "Reopen the Strait of Hormuz tomorrow, and the corn market still has a problem that doesn't resolve until 2027 at the earliest," said Hanley in a note. Gridlock at the Strait and damage to natural gas plants in the region won't be resolved overnight, and farmers will likely pay significantly higher prices to obtain fertilizer, or stretch whatever supply they have at the expense of strong crop yields.

Not Expecting Much: Thursday's WASDE report is not expected to sway markets, said AgResource in a note. "The USDA April crop report is offering support to CBOT grains, but [we doubt] that the USDA data will feed the bulls tomorrow," said the firm. Analysts surveyed by WSJ forecast that the USDA will reduce its outlook for U.S. soybean and wheat stockpiles, with corn stockpiles projected to rise 28 million bushels to 2.16 billion bushels.

INSIGHT

Hierarchy of Needs: Farmers can expect to feel higher prices in their crop budgets, said agricultural lender CoBank in a note. That's even with improved grain futures. "Improved commodity prices are not anticipated to offset higher input and production costs," said the firm. Instead, CoBank forecasts that "the sharp increase in diesel prices following the onset of the Iran war could add $2,000 in fuel costs per farmer and hundreds of thousands more for grain elevators."

Wide Range of Possibilities: If the climate shifts away from La Niña and into an El Niño system over the summer, as forecast by the NOAA, then a host of commodities are expected to feel the impact, said Jim Roemer of Best Weather. Heat and moisture in different regions of the world are directly linked to the success or failure of the incoming crop season. But it also may also be consequential for energy prices, said Roemer.

Looking Ahead: The sentiment of U.S. farmers improved in March, according to the Purdue University/CME Group Ag Economy Barometer, rising to 127 from 116 in February. Expectations for the year ahead continue to be cautiously optimistic, with 20% of respondents anticipating improved financial performance, compared with 18% expecting worse financial performance over the next 12 months. "While producers are feeling more optimistic about the future, there's still a noticeable gap between short-term challenges and long-term confidence," said Michael Langemeier, director of Purdue's Center for Commercial Agriculture.

AHEAD

-The USDA will release its weekly export sales report at 8:30 a.m. ET Thursday.

-The USDA will release its monthly WASDE report at noon ET Thursday.

-The CFTC will release its weekly Commitment of Traders report at 3:30 p.m. ET Friday.

Write to Kirk Maltais at kirk.maltais@wsj.com
